meta.question: Does milk consumption increase breast cancer risk?

positions.label: Evidence is inconsistent — no definitive conclusion on whether milk increases breast cancer risk
Milk/dairy consumption does not increase breast cancer risk
Milk consumption increases breast cancer risk via biological mechanisms

datasets.label: Japanese national mortality and dietary statistics (1947–1997)
Life After Cancer Epidemiology LACE study
pooled observational studies early life milk intake 7 breast cancer studies

factors.label: Soy milk substitution / mutual confounding
factors.rationale: Commentary highlights that apparent dairy-risk associations may be artefacts of soy displacement, a key confounding factor in studies with high plant-milk consumers.

provenance.quote: the observed association of dairy milk consumption with breast cancer risk may be confounded by soya milk consumption; those in the highest categories of dairy milk consumption consumed less soya milk, and vice versa.
Seventh-day Adventists often follow unique dietary patterns
Soy products were also frequently consumed in this population and were not associated with breast cancer. Substituting these for milk actually offered protection against the disease

factors.label: Vegan/vegetarian dietary pattern
factors.rationale: Non-dairy consumers are often vegans with independently lower risk, inflating any apparent protective effect of avoiding dairy.

provenance.quote: vegans may have a lower risk of breast cancer than non-vegetarians (22% lower risk)
vegans had a significantly lower lumbar spine BMD (6% lower; 95% CI: 2%, 9%), which was more pronounced than in lactoovovegetarians
Seventh-day Adventists often follow unique dietary patterns
The subjects were all Seventh Day Adventists with a large subset consuming either no dairy or little dairy thereby providing a significant group for comparison with the milk drinkers

factors.label: Life-period of dairy exposure (adolescent vs. adult)
factors.rationale: Study specifically examined adolescent through later-adulthood intake windows, finding no association at any life stage.

provenance.quote: the relationship may depend on the life-period of dietary assessment
Korean Women under 50 Years of Age

Although childhood and adolescence represent critical periods of rapid growth, the relationship between milk intake in early life and later cancer risk is unclear... No evidence of an association emerged in subgroup analyses of... life stage of milk intake

factors.label: Tumor subtype (ER/PR status)
factors.rationale: Analyses by subtype were conducted to assess whether associations differ by receptor status, addressing a key heterogeneity dimension.

provenance.quote: breast cancer by menopausal status and tumor molecular subtypes
especially for estrogen receptor-positive (ER+) (HR = 0.79, p = 0.002) and progesterone receptor-positive (PR+) BC (HR = 0.75, p = 0.027)
Subgroup analyses revealed differences by estrogen receptor (ER) status and menopausal stage.
No evidence of an association emerged in subgroup analyses of menopausal status, cancer stage

factors.label: Dairy fat content (high-fat vs. low-fat)
factors.rationale: Fat content of dairy modifies direction of effect, with high-fat trending toward harm and low-fat toward protection.

provenance.quote: High-fat dairy products are harmful to women, without statistical difference (HR = 1.06, 95%CI = 1.00–1.13, p = 0.066). On the contrary, low-fat dairy products can protect the premenopausal population (HR = 0.94)
Subgroup analyses based on limited numbers of studies sugge
Yogurt and low-fat dairy were generally associated with reduced breast cancer risk, while high-fat milk and processed cheese showed trends toward increased risk.
The associations with respect to other dairy products were not significant.
Dietary fat in dairy is a source of estrogenic hormones and may be related to worse breast cancer survival. We evaluated associations between high- and low-fat dairy intake, recurrence, and mortality
No evidence of an association emerged in subgroup analyses of menopausal status, cancer stage, fat content of milk, life stage of milk intake, or study design

factors.label: Fermentation status of dairy product
factors.rationale: Fermentation modifies the association, with benefits confined to postmenopausal women.

provenance.quote: Fermented dairy products can reduce BC risk in postmenopausal population (HR = 0.96), but have no protective effect on premenopausal population

factors.label: Exogenous estrogens in milk
factors.rationale: Paper focuses on mechanistic pathways by which cow's milk components (implied: hormones, growth factors) drive breast cancer initiation and progression.

provenance.quote: The Role of Cow's Milk Consumption in Breast Cancer Initiation and Progression
milk and dairy products should receive particular attention since they contain considerable amounts of estrogens.
Dietary fat in dairy is a source of estrogenic hormones and may be related to worse breast cancer survival.
Most are also pregnant and, by definition, lactating. So the milk they produce has increased levels of progestins and estrogen
Since the lactating cows are usually pregnant, they secrete estrogen and progesterone in the milk and these may play a role in triggering hormone-sensitive cancers

factors.label: IGF-1 / growth factor signalling from milk
factors.rationale: Title and mechanistic framing strongly suggest IGF-1 and related growth factors in milk are central proposed drivers of cancer risk.

provenance.quote: The Role of Cow's Milk Consumption in Breast Cancer Initiation and Progression
Taken together, these results provide the strongest evidence to date for a causal role of the IGF-pathway in breast cancer development
Milk also contains insulin-like growth factor 1, IGF-1, a protein that some studies have implicated as a possible stimulant of cancer cell proliferation

factors.label: Westernization of diet / animal food intake
factors.rationale: Broader shift to animal foods post-WWII is the ecological exposure; milk is highlighted within that shift but not isolated from other animal foods.

provenance.quote: the increase in the annual death rates from breast and ovarian cancers might be due to the lifestyle changes (increased consumption of animal-derived food) that occurred after 1945
